target.title: fix(codex): prefer native tokens for resume budget
target.body:
## Summary

This is the narrow follow-up for the remaining Codex app-server resume-budget edge case. Upstream `main` already has the broader native rollout guard and dynamic tool-result cap; this patch only changes token precedence when both native rollout usage and mirrored OpenClaw session totals are present.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: OpenClaw can keep a high mirrored session token total after Codex app-server compaction. Current `main` reads native `last_token_usage`, but still evaluates the resume guard with `max(sessionTokens, nativeTokens)`, so a stale mirrored total can rotate a healthy compacted native thread. This patch prefers `nativeTokens ?? sessionTokens`.

Real environment tested: Han local OpenClaw install on macOS, OpenClaw `2026.5.16-beta.3`, Gateway running against the main agent with WebChat/Codex app-server bindings.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Applied the same one-line runtime patch locally, restarted/checked OpenClaw, verified the installed runtime contains `const tokenCount = nativeTokens ?? sessionTokens;`, and ran the focused app-server regression lane in this rebased PR worktree.

Evidence after fix: Live terminal output from the local OpenClaw setup after the patch:

```text
$ rg -n "const tokenCount = nativeTokens \\?\\? sessionTokens" /usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/run-attempt-DEhr_oag.js
/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/run-attempt-DEhr_oag.js:2543:\tconst tokenCount = nativeTokens ?? sessionTokens;

$ openclaw health --json | jq '{ok,eventLoop:.eventLoop,telegram:.channels.telegram.connected}'
{
  "ok": true,
  "eventLoop": {
    "degraded": false,
    "reasons": [],
    "delayP99Ms": 22.8,
    "utilization": 0.021,
    "cpuCoreRatio": 0.022
  },
  "telegram": true
}
```

Observed result after fix: The local installed runtime now uses native Codex rollout token usage first, so a compacted native thread is not forced to rotate solely because OpenClaw mirrored session totals are stale. Gateway stayed healthy after the patch. The rebased PR regression also passed: `Test Files 1 passed (1); Tests 7 passed | 151 skipped (158)`.

What was not tested: Full repository test suite and a live upstream CI WebChat session. The live local Gateway/runtime check and focused app-server regression were tested.

## Supplemental validation

```bash
node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts
git diff --check
tmp=$(mktemp); printf '%s\n' '["codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts"]' > "$tmp"
OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE="$tmp" OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts --testNamePattern 'native rollout|stale mirrored'
```

Supplemental results: oxfmt passed, git diff whitespace passed, focused Vitest passed.

target.title: fix: clear cross-run messaging-tool sent-text state after every compaction
target.body:
## Problem

When a run completes and the session compacts, the arrays tracking texts sent via messaging tools (`message(action=send)`, `sessions_send`, etc.) were only cleared on compaction retries (`willRetry=true`). On normal compaction (`willRetry=false`), stale entries survived into the next run.

`isMessagingToolDuplicateNormalized()` compares the final reply text against these stale entries using substring matching (`normalized.includes(normalizedSent)`). If the new run's text happens to share a normalized substring with any entry from the previous run, the block reply is silently dropped with:

> Skipping message_end block reply - already sent via messaging tool

## Fix

Clear `messagingToolSentTexts`, `messagingToolSentTextsNormalized`, `messagingToolSentTargets`, and `messagingToolSentMediaUrls` unconditionally in the `compaction_end` handler — before the `willRetry` branch — so the next run always starts with a clean slate.

## Verification

- Relevant tests pass (6/6) in `pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.compaction.test.ts` (updated fixture)
- No logic change to `emitBlockChunk`, `handleMessageEnd`, or the dedup function itself — change is purely about state lifecycle between runs

# Microsoft Ecosystem Issues & PRs Tracker

> **Purpose:** Living checklist for maintainers to track all open Microsoft-related issues and PRs (Teams, Windows, WSL, Azure, M365/SharePoint).
>
> **How to use:**
>
> - Mark items resolved by editing this PR body and changing `[ ]` to `[x]`
> - Claim items by adding your GitHub handle to the `Assignee` column
> - Priority guide: **P0** = crash/blocker/security, **P1** = significant bug/regression, **P2** = minor bug/enhancement, **P3** = nice-to-have/stale
> - Items marked _(stale)_ have been flagged by the stale bot due to inactivity

Reproducibility: Partially yes: the source-level helper reproduction is high-confidence because current main returns `process-default` for Node 22 plus `defaultResultOrder: "verbatim"` before reaching the Telegram `ipv4first` fallback. target.title: fix(telegram): keep Node 22 Bot API DNS IPv4-first
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Telegram Bot API calls on Node 22 can still inherit a process-wide DNS result order of `verbatim`, letting broken IPv6 routes win even though Telegram has a Node 22 IPv4-first default.
- Why it matters: On WSL2/systemd hosts where IPv6 to `api.telegram.org` returns `ENETUNREACH` but IPv4 works, bootstrap calls like `getMe` can fail before the bot initializes.
- What changed: Telegram DNS resolution now keeps explicit Telegram env/config overrides first, honors a process default that already prefers IPv4, and otherwise applies the Node 22 Telegram-specific `ipv4first` default before falling back to older process defaults.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No credential, token, proxy, service, webhook, polling, or global DNS configuration changes.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #82994
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

External contributors must show after-fix evidence from a real OpenClaw setup. Unit tests, mocks, lint, typechecks, snapshots, and CI are supplemental only. Screenshots are encouraged even for CLI, console, text, or log changes; terminal screenshots and copied live output count. Be mindful of private information like IP addresses, API keys, phone numbers, non-public endpoints, or other private details when providing evidence.

- Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram Bot API transport should prefer IPv4 on Node 22 even when the process default DNS result order is `verbatim`.
- Real environment tested: Local macOS development checkout, Node test harness only.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: See Repro + Verification below.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Unit red/green evidence: the new regression test failed before the source change with received `{ value: "verbatim", source: "process-default" }`, then passed after the fix.
- Observed result after fix: Focused Telegram network, fetch, probe, and polling tests pass; extension TypeScript check passes.
- What was not tested: Live WSL2/systemd Telegram bot startup with a real bot token and intentionally broken IPv6 route.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/network-config.test.ts --reporter=verbose` failed the new test before implementation: expected `ipv4first/default-node22`, received `verbatim/process-default`.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The Telegram DNS decision helper accepted `dns.getDefaultResultOrder() === "verbatim"` before applying its Node 22 Telegram-specific `ipv4first` default, so a process-wide default could bypass the Telegram IPv4 mitigation.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing tests covered Node 22 defaulting to `ipv4first` only when there was no process default, but not when the process default was `verbatim`.
- Contributing context (if known): The issue report describes WSL2/systemd with Node v22.22.2 where Node/undici gets `ENETUNREACH` to Telegram while curl/Python can reach the IPv4 path.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/telegram/src/network-config.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: On Node 22, a process default of `verbatim` does not override Telegram's `ipv4first` default.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The regression is in the pure DNS decision function, before transport dispatchers are constructed.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing fallback transport tests cover sticky IPv4 fallback and probe forceFallback, but not this process-default precedence gap.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Telegram Bot API calls on Node 22 prefer IPv4 by default unless explicitly overridden by Telegram env/config. This is scoped to Telegram network configuration.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[Node 22 + process DNS verbatim] -> [Telegram inherits verbatim] -> [IPv6 may win] -> [ENETUNREACH]

After:
[Node 22 + process DNS verbatim] -> [Telegram default ipv4first] -> [IPv4 preferred] -> [Bot API reachable when IPv4 works]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS local development checkout
- Runtime/container: Node/Vitest via repository scripts
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram extension
- Relevant config (redacted): No live Telegram token or service config used

### Steps

1. Add a regression test for Node 22 with `defaultResultOrder: "verbatim"`.
2. Run the test before implementation and observe the failure.
3. Update the Telegram DNS precedence.
4. Run focused Telegram tests and extension typecheck.

### Expected

- Telegram Node 22 DNS decision returns `{ value: "ipv4first", source: "default-node22" }` when process default is `verbatim`.

### Actual

- Before fix: returned `{ value: "verbatim", source: "process-default" }`.
- After fix: returns `{ value: "ipv4first", source: "default-node22" }`.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: Red test failed before implementation; focused Telegram suite passed after implementation; extension typecheck passed after implementation.
- Edge cases checked: Env/config overrides still take priority; process `ipv4first` is still honored; older Node can still inherit process `verbatim`.
- What you did **not** verify: Live WSL2/systemd Telegram startup/send/receive against a real bot token with broken IPv6 route.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A user relying on a process-wide `verbatim` default for Telegram on Node 22 will now get Telegram's IPv4-first default.
  - Mitigation: Explicit Telegram env/config `dnsResultOrder: "verbatim"` still wins when deliberately configured.

target.title: fix: add resilient fallback policy for user model overrides
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: session/user model overrides always disabled configured fallback chains.
- Why it matters: operators could configure provider fallback behavior, but a user-selected model had no fallback safety net during transient provider/model failures.
- What changed: adds opt-in `userOverrideFallbackPolicy: "resilient"` on `agents.defaults.model` and per-agent chat model config, with schema/types/help/labels/docs and fallback-resolution tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): default behavior remains `strict`; auto-selected model overrides and configured agent primaries keep their existing fallback behavior; non-chat model config schemas remain unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: a user/session model override can now inherit the configured fallback chain when the new policy is explicitly set to `resilient`, while the default `strict` behavior still returns no fallbacks.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on pop-os, Node v25.8.1, branch `fix/user-override-fallback-policy`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```bash
  pnpm exec tsx -e 'import { resolveEffectiveModelFallbacks } from "./src/agents/agent-scope.ts"; const cfg={agents:{defaults:{model:{primary:"openai/gpt-5.4",fallbacks:["anthropic/claude-haiku-3-5","google/gemini-2.5-flash"],userOverrideFallbackPolicy:"resilient"}},list:[{id:"linus"}]}}; console.log("user override resilient =>", JSON.stringify(resolveEffectiveModelFallbacks({cfg,agentId:"linus",hasSessionModelOverride:true,modelOverrideSource:"user"}))); const strict={agents:{defaults:{model:{primary:"openai/gpt-5.4",fallbacks:["anthropic/claude-haiku-3-5"]}},list:[{id:"linus"}]}}; console.log("user override strict =>", JSON.stringify(resolveEffectiveModelFallbacks({cfg:strict,agentId:"linus",hasSessionModelOverride:true,modelOverrideSource:"user"})));'
  ```
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  ```text
  user override resilient => ["anthropic/claude-haiku-3-5","google/gemini-2.5-flash"]
  user override strict => []
  ```
- Observed result after fix: resilient user overrides receive the configured fallback list; strict user overrides receive an empty fallback list.
- What was not tested: live provider failover against real external model APIs, because that would require changing a running gateway config. The changed resolver and config schema were tested locally.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): covered by the existing and new regression tests: the pre-change resolver returned `[]` for user overrides whenever `hasSessionModelOverride` was true.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: `resolveEffectiveModelFallbacks` treated every non-auto session model override as authoritative and returned an empty fallback list without a config escape hatch.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no regression coverage for user-selected models that should intentionally keep operator-configured fallback chains.
- Contributing context (if known): auto model overrides and user/session overrides were represented in the same resolver path, but operators need separate fallback policy for user choices.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts`, `src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: user model overrides remain strict by default, can opt into resilient fallback inheritance, and per-agent policy overrides defaults.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the behavior is selected in the config schema and resolved in the agent-scope fallback helper before provider execution.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users can set `userOverrideFallbackPolicy: "resilient"` under `agents.defaults.model` or an individual agent's chat `model` block. The default is still `strict`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[user override] -> [fallbacks disabled]

After:
[user override] -> [strict policy] -> [fallbacks disabled]
[user override] -> [resilient policy] -> [configured fallbacks preserved]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: pop-os Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node v25.8.1 / pnpm
- Model/provider: N/A, pure config + fallback resolver change
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): `agents.defaults.model.userOverrideFallbackPolicy: "resilient"`

### Steps

1. Configure fallback models under `agents.defaults.model.fallbacks`.
2. Set `agents.defaults.model.userOverrideFallbackPolicy: "resilient"`.
3. Resolve fallbacks for a session/user model override.

### Expected

- Resilient user overrides inherit configured fallback models.
- Strict/default user overrides keep fallback models disabled.

### Actual

- Matches expected in the local resolver smoke and targeted tests.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Verification commands run:

```text
codex review --base origin/main
pnpm check:changed
pnpm test src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts
pnpm config:docs:check && pnpm config:schema:check
git diff --check origin/main...HEAD
```

`codex review --base origin/main` result:

```text
The change consistently adds the new user override fallback policy across config types, schema validation, labels/help, docs, and fallback resolution. I did not find a discrete correctness issue in the modified paths.
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: strict default, resilient defaults, per-agent policy override, explicit fallback override behavior, config schema acceptance/rejection, generated config docs/schema consistency.
- Edge cases checked: explicit empty fallback list still disables fallback inheritance; non-chat model schemas still reject the chat-only policy field.
- What you did **not** verify: live external provider outage failover through a running gateway.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

target.title: Feature/exec denylist
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: This PR introduces a new feature - exec denylist. this is done to bridge the gap between unusable allowlist where the agent asks approval for every single command it runs and full on YOLO agent can do everything unrestricted. The feature sets some boundary for the agent so we don't have agent bypass things like web_search tool and run curl/wget instead and get prompt injected. - Problem: - Why it matters: safer than YOLO - What changed: added exec denylist feature - What did NOT change (scope boundary): other features ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior addressed: exec denylist blocks model-initiated shell network fetches (`curl`/`wget`) in a real gateway run, preventing a headless agent from bypassing safer fetch tooling. - Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw gateway and Control UI exec approvals on macOS, running an Ollama `qwen3.6:35b-a3b-q4_K_M` scheduled cron agent with effective `tools.exec` security resolved to `denylist`. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: Configured exec approvals to use denylist, ran the scheduled `AI Lab Morning Brief` cron job, then inspected live gateway logs and the persisted runtime transcript with `openclaw logs --limit 300 --plain` and the matching session transcript. - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Screenshots above plus copied redacted live runtime output below. - Observed result after fix: Seven attempted `exec` commands containing `curl` were denied by rule `default-shell-network-fetch`; the model received `exec command is denied due to command in deny list`, then switched to `web_fetch` and completed the cron run instead of bypassing the safer fetch path. - What was not tested: content:notTested - This proof did not verify iMessage delivery; the cron delivery later timed out on the local iMessage bridge, unrelated to exec denylist behavior. It also did not test every possible custom deny rule shape beyond the default shell network-fetch rule. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Redacted runtime evidence: ```text 2026-05-17T11:15:14.803Z info exec denylist: denied command hash=f65c003a792cfdc0 length=100 host=gateway agent=main trigger=cron ruleIndex=0 2026-05-17T11:15:14.804Z info exec denylist: denied command hash=16c1f70c7f7d90e6 length=655 host=gateway agent=main trigger=cron ruleIndex=0 2026-05-17T11:15:14.805Z info exec denylist: denied command hash=10a2a034cfd89748 length=125 host=gateway agent=main trigger=cron ruleIndex=0 2026-05-17T11:15:14.806Z info exec denylist: denied command hash=72a121ccd1eeb9f2 length=126 host=gateway agent=main trigger=cron ruleIndex=0 2026-05-17T11:15:14.807Z info exec denylist: denied command hash=a0ea71b5b6367cd0 length=169 host=gateway agent=main trigger=cron ruleIndex=0 2026-05-17T11:15:14.808Z info exec denylist: denied command hash=1b2a1a8991ec2015 length=123 host=gateway agent=main trigger=cron ruleIndex=0 2026-05-17T11:15:14.809Z info exec denylist: denied command hash=cc66320e783f16f7 length=527 host=gateway agent=main trigger=cron ruleIndex=0 redacted transcript: toolResult x7 -> exec command is denied due to command in deny list redacted transcript: assistant -> Exec is blocked on this run. Let me use web_fetch for the heavy lifting. cron result: finished status=ok durationMs=90606 model=qwen3.6:35b-a3b-q4_K_M provider=ollama ``` ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) Tests were written for this, but for validation so deny mode is synthetic ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Denies undesired exec runs List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). Denylist is now an option in exec security configuration: Control UI -> Nodes -> Exec approvals -> ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`) - New/changed network calls? (`No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes`) - Data access scope changed? (`No`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: This actually shrinks the YOLO execution surface... ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: MacOS Tahoe Version 26.4.1 (25E253) - Runtime/container: node - Model/provider: Qwen 3.6 35b/ollama - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): denylist ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: see screenshots - Edge cases checked: not sure what edge case i would need for a deny list - What you did **not** verify: other features ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. - Shadow told me to do it... If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes`) - Config/env changes? (`Yes`) - Migration needed? (`No`) - If yes, exact upgrade steps: ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: None - Mitigation: None

target.title: gateway: support user-role chat.inject with idempotent replays
target.body:
## Summary
- extend `chat.inject` params with optional `role` (`assistant` | `user`) and `idempotencyKey`
- preserve existing assistant injection behavior as default
- add a user-role transcript append path through the shared transcript append pipeline
- make idempotent `chat.inject` replays return `{ ok: true, deduped: true }` instead of failing
- add unit coverage for injected user transcript append

## Why
Telegram relay hooks need a first-class Gateway RPC to append user-role messages into a session transcript without triggering a model run.

## Notes
- backward compatible: existing `chat.inject` calls without `role` remain assistant injections
- no scope changes required (still under existing `chat.inject` authorization)

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: `chat.inject` must support user-role transcript append and idempotent replay should not fail.
- Real environment tested: Live OpenClaw Gateway on Linux host, local operator runtime, real session transcript files under `~/.openclaw/agents/*/sessions/*.jsonl`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. `openclaw gateway call chat.inject --json --timeout 30000 --params '{"sessionKey":"agent:druck:telegram:group:-1003846579956:topic:1","role":"user","message":"REAL_PROOF_ROLE_USER_2026-05-13","idempotencyKey":"real-proof-user-20260513-c"}'`
  2. Repeat same command with same `idempotencyKey`.
  3. `rg -n 'real-proof-user-20260513-c|REAL_PROOF_ROLE_USER_2026-05-13' /home/aaron/.openclaw/agents/druck/sessions/d0b805cb-c944-4079-9351-3d9b620a76d2-topic-1.jsonl && rg -c 'real-proof-user-20260513-c' /home/aaron/.openclaw/agents/druck/sessions/d0b805cb-c944-4079-9351-3d9b620a76d2-topic-1.jsonl`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
```text
$ openclaw gateway call chat.inject --json --timeout 30000 --params '{"sessionKey":"agent:druck:telegram:group:-1003846579956:topic:1","role":"user","message":"REAL_PROOF_ROLE_USER_2026-05-13","idempotencyKey":"real-proof-user-20260513-c"}'
{
  "ok": true,
  "messageId": "c7b247bc"
}

$ openclaw gateway call chat.inject --json --timeout 30000 --params '{"sessionKey":"agent:druck:telegram:group:-1003846579956:topic:1","role":"user","message":"REAL_PROOF_ROLE_USER_2026-05-13","idempotencyKey":"real-proof-user-20260513-c"}'
{
  "ok": true,
  "deduped": true
}

$ rg -n 'real-proof-user-20260513-c|REAL_PROOF_ROLE_USER_2026-05-13' /home/aaron/.openclaw/agents/druck/sessions/d0b805cb-c944-4079-9351-3d9b620a76d2-topic-1.jsonl
827:{"type":"message","id":"c7b247bc",...,"message":{"role":"user","content":[{"type":"text","text":"REAL_PROOF_ROLE_USER_2026-05-13"}],...,"idempotencyKey":"real-proof-user-20260513-c"}}

$ rg -c 'real-proof-user-20260513-c' /home/aaron/.openclaw/agents/druck/sessions/d0b805cb-c944-4079-9351-3d9b620a76d2-topic-1.jsonl
1
```
- Observed result after fix: First call appends a user-role transcript message; second call with same `idempotencyKey` returns deduped success; transcript persists exactly one matching entry.
- What was not tested: Full end-to-end Telegram channel mention loop was not re-run as part of this proof block.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this patch, replaying the same idempotency key returned `failed to write transcript: unknown error` for this flow.

## Hardening: race-safe idempotency (commit 9ea70b8681)

Third-party review flagged that the original `transcriptHasIdempotencyKey` precheck happened **before** acquiring the per-transcript queue+write lock — a TOCTOU window where two concurrent identical requests could both pass the check and persist duplicates. Fixed by moving the dedupe scan **inside** the locked critical section in `appendSessionTranscriptMessage` and threading `idempotencyKey` through as a first-class parameter. Result is returned with `deduped: true` and the canonical existing `messageId`, so duplicate broadcasts are suppressed too.

### Concurrent replay test (added in this PR)
`src/gateway/server-methods/chat.inject.parentid.test.ts` — 16-way `Promise.all` of identical idempotent injects:

```text
✓  gateway-methods  src/gateway/server-methods/chat.inject.parentid.test.ts (5 tests) 837ms
   ✓ dedupes concurrent identical idempotent injects (race-safe)
```

Asserts: `messageIds.size === 1`, `dedupedCount === concurrency - 1`, and the on-disk transcript contains exactly one entry with the matching `idempotencyKey`.

### Live concurrent evidence (8-way, hot-patched runtime)
```text
$ IDEM="race-final-1778710992"
$ for i in 1..8; do (openclaw gateway call chat.inject \
    --params '{"sessionKey":"...","role":"user","message":"race final","idempotencyKey":"'$IDEM'"}' \
    </dev/null >/tmp/race-$i.out 2>&1) & done; wait

[1] {"ok":true,"deduped":true}
[2] {"ok":true,"deduped":true}
[3] {"ok":true,"deduped":true}
[4] {"ok":true,"deduped":true}
[5] {"ok":true,"deduped":true}
[6] {"ok":true,"deduped":true}
[7] {"ok":true,"deduped":true}
[8] {"ok":true,"deduped":true}

# disk-state invariant: at-most-once persistence
$ grep -c "$IDEM" $TRANSCRIPT
1
$ grep "$IDEM" $TRANSCRIPT | jq -r .id | sort -u | wc -l
1
```

The on-disk invariant — at most one persisted entry per `(transcriptPath, idempotencyKey)` — holds under true concurrency.


## Post-update verification (2026-05-15)

Additional in-depth edge-case coverage was added after initial hardening:

- no-idempotency-key path does not dedupe (two writes persist)
- deduped replay does not emit duplicate transcript-update events
- assistant-role inject dedupes correctly by idempotency key
- distinct idempotency keys remain isolated (no cross-key collision)

Updated local test results on branch head `0d8ea32769`:

```text
✓  plugins  src/plugins/registry.runtime-config.test.ts (1 test)
✓  gateway-client  src/gateway/server-methods/chat.inject.parentid.test.ts (10 tests)
✓  gateway-methods  src/gateway/server-methods/chat.inject.parentid.test.ts (10 tests)
Tests  21 passed (21)
```

target.title: feat: add scoped mention pattern policy
target.body:
## Summary
- add a shared scoped mention-pattern policy for global, agent, and provider/channel overrides
- apply configured mention-pattern gating across channel runtimes while preserving native mentions
- document raw regex-body syntax and regenerate config/help metadata

## Notes
- Recreated from a clean `origin/main` branch after #70846 was closed as dirty; this branch has been rebased on current `origin/main` and generated baselines refreshed after upstream movement.

## Validation
- `pnpm --config.minimum-release-age=0 install --lockfile-only --ignore-scripts=false --config.engine-strict=false --config.enable-pre-post-scripts=true --config.side-effects-cache=true`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- `pnpm config:channels:check`
- `pnpm test extensions/qa-channel/src/inbound.test.ts src/channels/mention-pattern-policy.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.body.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Scoped mention-pattern policy now resolves the effective provider/agent/conversation policy so configured regex mentions are enabled only for explicitly allowed conversations in deny mode and remain disabled for denied or unlisted conversations. Latest follow-up rebased onto current `origin/main`, resolved refreshed generated config/plugin SDK baseline conflicts, and reran the exact-head behavior proof.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw PR checkout on host `patrick`, branch `feature/mention-pattern-policy-clean`, head `40b87a01ad31093073f361b68f2a866c5af76452`, running the real source resolver, Telegram group peer id helper, mention matcher, and inbound mention decision code through Node/tsx against redacted OpenClaw-style config/message facts. This does not exercise live external delivery.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `cd /home/patrick/projects/openclaw-mention-pattern-policy && HEAD_SHA=40b87a01ad31093073f361b68f2a866c5af76452 HEAD_SHORT=40b87a01ad node --import tsx /tmp/pr70864-proof-02d093.mjs`
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the real source smoke:

```text
OpenClaw scoped mention policy Telegram account media pre-download proof (40b87a01ad, 40b87a01ad31093073f361b68f2a866c5af76452)
{"topic":7,"conversationId":"-1001234567893:topic:7","accountPolicyAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:7"],"globalProviderAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:global-only"],"mentionPatternsEnabled":true,"wasMentioned":true,"shouldSkip":false,"skipReason":null}
{"topic":9,"conversationId":"-1001234567893:topic:9","accountPolicyAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:7"],"globalProviderAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:global-only"],"mentionPatternsEnabled":false,"wasMentioned":false,"shouldSkip":true,"skipReason":null}
{"topic":11,"conversationId":"-1001234567893:topic:11","accountPolicyAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:7"],"globalProviderAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:global-only"],"mentionPatternsEnabled":false,"wasMentioned":false,"shouldSkip":true,"skipReason":null}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** In inherited `deny` mode, the media pre-download path honors the Telegram account policy rather than the global provider fallback: account-allowed topic `-1001234567893:topic:7` enables configured regex mention `bot` and media pre-download is not skipped, while account-denied topic `:topic:9` and unlisted topic `:topic:11` disable configured regex mentions and skip unaddressed group media before download.
- **What was not tested:** Full live external Telegram delivery was not exercised in this proof; CI and targeted channel tests cover the integration paths.

target.title: feat: pass requesterSenderId and senderIsOwner to ChannelAgentToolFactory
target.body:
## Summary

Pass trusted sender context (`requesterSenderId` and `senderIsOwner`) to `ChannelAgentToolFactory`, enabling channel plugins to implement sender-aware access control in their agent tools.

## Problem

`listChannelAgentTools()` only passes `{ cfg }` to channel agent tool factories, while the immediately following `createOpenClawTools()` call in the same function receives the full sender context. This gap means channel-registered agent tools cannot distinguish between different senders.

## Changes

**3 files changed, +17 / -3**

### `src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts`
- Extended `ChannelAgentToolFactory` params type to include `requesterSenderId?: string | null` and `senderIsOwner?: boolean`

### `src/agents/channel-tools.ts`
- Updated `listChannelAgentTools` function signature to accept the new fields
- These are already forwarded to the factory via `entry(params)`

### `src/agents/pi-tools.ts`
- Pass `options?.senderId` and `options?.senderIsOwner` from the existing `options` object into `listChannelAgentTools()`

## Backward Compatibility

Fully backward compatible. Existing factories that only destructure `{ cfg }` will simply ignore the new optional fields. No changes required for any existing channel plugin.

## Use Case

The DMWork channel plugin needs sender-aware agent tools where:
- Any user can query their own DM history with the bot
- Any user can query groups they are a member of
- Only the bot owner can query other users' DM history

Without sender context in the factory, the plugin must choose between `ownerOnly: true` (too restrictive) or no access control (too permissive).

Closes #58806

target.title: fix(agents): preserve run-mode keep subagents past session sweep TTL (#83132)
target.body:
## Summary

Refs #83132. Companion to #83146.

The sweeper in `src/agents/subagent-registry.ts` applies `SESSION_RUN_TTL_MS` (5 minutes after `cleanupCompletedAt`) to any registry entry with `archiveAtMs === undefined`. The comment claims this is for session-mode runs, but `archiveAtMs` is **also** undefined by design for **run-mode** subagents spawned with `cleanup: "keep"` (`subagent-registry-run-manager.ts:475-480`):

```ts
const archiveAtMs =
  spawnMode === "session" || registerParams.cleanup === "keep"
    ? undefined
    : archiveAfterMs
      ? now + archiveAfterMs
      : undefined;
```

Result: a `cleanup: "keep"` run-mode subagent finishes → `cleanupCompletedAt` set → five minutes later the sweeper deletes it from `subagents/runs.json` while sessions/transcripts stay on disk. This matches the symptom in #83132 — *"five accepted native subagent children whose sessions were present in the session store, but none of the five run IDs appeared in `~/.openclaw/subagents/runs.json`"* — the reporter explicitly used `runtime:"subagent"`, `mode:"run"`, `cleanup:"keep"`.

Fix: gate the absolute-TTL prune so it does not apply to non-session `cleanup: "keep"` entries.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Issue #83132 — `cleanup: "keep"` run-mode subagents disappear from `~/.openclaw/subagents/runs.json` 5 minutes after cleanup completes, despite `keep` meaning "retain". After this patch they stay registered.

- **Real environment tested:** Local checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` on `/private/tmp/openclaw`. macOS 25.3.0 (Darwin arm64). `node v25.9.0`, `pnpm 11.1.0`. `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` clean. Live demo loads the real `src/agents/subagent-registry.ts` via `tsx` and runs the real sweeper against a real `~/.openclaw/subagents/runs.json` on a fresh `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` tempdir — no vitest, no fake timers, no mocked persistence. Only the external gateway/announce dependencies are stubbed because there is no live agent to call.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. `git checkout fix/subagent-sweeper-keep-mode-ttl-83132`
  2. `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
  3. Drop a local-only repro file at `scripts/repro-83132-sweep.mjs` (not committed) that:
     - sets `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` to a fresh tempdir,
     - imports `src/agents/subagent-registry.ts` via tsx,
     - registers a run-mode `cleanup: "keep"` entry whose `cleanupCompletedAt` is back-dated 10 minutes (twice the `SESSION_RUN_TTL_MS = 5 min` cutoff),
     - calls `__testing.sweepOnceForTests()` against the real sweeper,
     - reads back the in-memory list and the on-disk `runs.json`.
  4. `node --import tsx scripts/repro-83132-sweep.mjs`
  5. `git checkout main` and repeat step 4 against unmodified main to capture the pre-fix behavior.

- **Evidence after fix:** Copied live terminal output from both invocations, captured from my local shell on `/private/tmp/openclaw`. The script writes the entry to disk via `saveSubagentRegistryToDisk` before sweeping, so the after-sweep disk check is a real persisted-registry observation. Stdout reproduced verbatim.

  **After patch (`fix/subagent-sweeper-keep-mode-ttl-83132`):**
  ```
  $ node --import tsx scripts/repro-83132-sweep.mjs
  --- before sweep ---
  registry: live-run-keep-run-mode
  disk /var/folders/.../openclaw-sweep-repro-i16CDM/subagents/runs.json:
  {
    "version": 2,
    "runs": {
      "live-run-keep-run-mode": {
        "runId": "live-run-keep-run-mode",
        "childSessionKey": "agent:main:subagent:live-keep",
        "requesterSessionKey": "agent:main:main",
        "requesterDisplayKey": "main",
        "task": "live keep-mode run-mode subagent",
        "cleanup": "keep",
        "spawnMode": "run",
        "createdAt": 1779034336836,
        "startedAt": 1779034336836,
        "endedAt": 1779034337836,
        "cleanupHandled": true,
        "cleanupCompletedAt": 1779034337836,
        "outcome": { "status": "ok", "startedAt": 1779034336836, "endedAt": 1779034337836, "elapsedMs": 1000 }
      }
    }
  }
  cleanupCompletedAt age: 10.00 min (SESSION_RUN_TTL_MS = 5 min)
  --- after sweep ---
  registry: live-run-keep-run-mode
  disk /var/folders/.../openclaw-sweep-repro-i16CDM/subagents/runs.json:
  {
    "version": 2,
    "runs": {
      "live-run-keep-run-mode": {
        "runId": "live-run-keep-run-mode",
        "childSessionKey": "agent:main:subagent:live-keep",
        ...
        "cleanupCompletedAt": 1779034337836,
        "outcome": { "status": "ok", ... }
      }
    }
  }
  RESULT: PASS — entry survived sweep both in-memory and on-disk
  ```

  **Before patch (`main` at `833f1ce7`), same script, same setup:**
  ```
  $ node --import tsx scripts/repro-83132-sweep.mjs
  --- before sweep ---
  registry: live-run-keep-run-mode
  disk /var/folders/.../openclaw-sweep-repro-hZiHkV/subagents/runs.json:
  {
    "version": 2,
    "runs": {
      "live-run-keep-run-mode": { ... "cleanup": "keep", "spawnMode": "run", "cleanupCompletedAt": 1779034347260, ... }
    }
  }
  cleanupCompletedAt age: 10.00 min (SESSION_RUN_TTL_MS = 5 min)
  --- after sweep ---
  registry: (none)
  disk /var/folders/.../openclaw-sweep-repro-hZiHkV/subagents/runs.json:
  {
    "version": 2,
    "runs": {}
  }

  RESULT: FAIL — entry pruned from both in-memory and on-disk
  ```

  The post-sweep on-disk `runs.json` `{ "version": 2, "runs": {} }` on `main` is exactly the symptom in the bug report: *"none of the five run IDs appeared in `~/.openclaw/subagents/runs.json`"*. After the patch, the sweeper leaves both the in-memory entry and the on-disk row untouched.

- **Observed result after fix:** With the patch, `cleanup: "keep"` + `spawnMode: "run"` entries survive the real sweeper past 10 minutes (twice the prior TTL window). Both the in-memory `listSubagentRunsForRequester` result and the persisted `~/.openclaw/subagents/runs.json` file still contain the run row. Pre-fix, the same setup leaves an empty `runs.json` on disk and the list returns nothing for that runId. Session-mode (`spawnMode: "session"`) runs still get pruned 5 minutes after cleanup — the gate only exempts the non-session keep path.

- **What was not tested:** I did not exercise the original live QQ / Discord embedded-channel five-children wave from the bug report (no local QQ harness). I also did not run the full `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` end-to-end locally; CI on this PR re-runs that.

### Supplemental regression coverage (mocks, not the proof)

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  56 passed (56)
```

New regression case in `subagent-registry.test.ts` ("preserves run-mode keep entries past SESSION_RUN_TTL_MS sweep") fails on `main`, passes on this branch.

## Why not in #83146

#83146 lands the narrow "fail-closed initial registration" durability fix Clawsweeper recommended. This PR is a separate, smaller surface (one-line gate on the sweeper + one regression test) that targets the actual root-cause path for the reporter's exact `cleanup:"keep"` scenario. Keeping it isolated so each fix can be reviewed and reverted independently.

## Test plan

- [x] `node --import tsx scripts/repro-83132-sweep.mjs` on the fix branch (local repro file, not committed) — see Evidence above
- [x] Same script on `main` to capture pre-fix prune behavior — see Evidence above
- [x] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts`
- [ ] CI: full `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` lane on this PR

target.title: Fix denied Codex app-server tool policy
target.body:
## Summary
- Honor runtime `toolsAllow` restrictions in the Codex app-server by disabling native code-mode config and app-server plugin apps whenever the allowlist is explicitly restricted.
- Treat `toolsAllow: []` as an empty Codex dynamic-tool catalog while preserving wildcard and forced-message behavior.

## Root Cause
Telegram `toolsBySender` deny-all resolves to `toolsAllow: []` for the run. The Codex app-server still always sent `features.code_mode=true`, could still enable Codex plugin app config, and its dynamic tool allowlist helper treated an empty allowlist as unrestricted. That let a denied sender start a run with `toolCount: 0` while Codex-native `bash` / `apply_patch` remained available.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: A runtime sender policy that resolves to `toolsAllow: []` no longer exposes Codex-native code tools, Codex dynamic tools, or Codex app-server plugin apps.

Real environment tested: Local temp worktree from current `origin/main`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts`

Evidence after fix: `Test Files 2 passed (2)` and `Tests 153 passed (153)`.

Observed result after fix: The new run-attempt regression observes `thread/start` receives `dynamicTools: []`, `features.code_mode: false`, `features.code_mode_only: false`, no enabled Google Calendar app, and no `app/list` request when `toolsAllow` is empty.

What was not tested: This branch was not deployed into the live Telegram/EVE container.

## Verification
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts`
- `codex review --uncommitted` (no actionable correctness issues found; the review tool reported its own sandbox could not verify local filesystem state, so local `git`/test commands above covered that)

## What was not tested
- Live Telegram roundtrip against Jackie/EVE with this branch installed.
- Broad `pnpm check` / full CI locally.

target.title: feat(anthropic): claude-cli-interactive backend — stream reasoning via local TLS proxy (experimental)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Motivation (dated, citable).** On **May 13, 2026** Anthropic announced that, starting **June 15, 2026**, Claude subscriptions split into two pools: **interactive usage stays on existing subscription limits** (Claude Code, Cowork, claude.ai), while **programmatic usage moves onto a separate fixed monthly credit pool** — explicitly enumerated as Agent SDK, `claude -p` (the non-interactive command), Claude Code GitHub Actions, **and third-party apps that auth with the subscription through the Agent SDK** (i.e. OpenClaw on the existing `claude-cli` backend). The programmatic credit is fixed per tier (Pro $20 / Max 5x $100 / Max 20x $200 / Team $100/seat / Enterprise $200/seat), billed at API rates, and does **not** roll over. Coverage: [VentureBeat — "Anthropic reinstates OpenClaw and third-party agent usage on Claude subscriptions — with a catch"](https://venturebeat.com/technology/anthropic-reinstates-openclaw-and-third-party-agent-usage-on-claude-subscriptions-with-a-catch), [SiliconANGLE — "Anthropic announces ‘programmatic credit pool’ as agentic tool use rises"](https://siliconangle.com/2026/05/14/anthropic-announces-programmatic-credit-pool-agentic-tool-use-rises/).
- **Why this PR exists.** OpenClaw's existing `claude-cli` backend invokes `claude -p --output-format stream-json` — i.e. exactly the non-interactive command the announcement names as moving to the programmatic credit pool on June 15. After June 15 every OpenClaw turn on a Claude subscription will drain the fixed credit pool at API rates rather than count against the user's interactive subscription limits, and Opus turns in particular will exhaust the credit very quickly. This PR adds a second backend that drives `claude` in interactive mode (no `-p`) so OpenClaw stays on the interactive pool that subscribers are actually paying for.
- **What changed.** A new `claude-cli-interactive` backend runs `claude` without `-p` (interactive mode) under a per-turn `bun` wrapper that boots a loopback HTTPS MITM proxy, taps every API SSE event to reconstruct `claude -p --output-format stream-json` JSONL records on its own stdout, and kills the claude child on `end_turn`. A `tty-spoof.cjs` `NODE_OPTIONS=--require` preload tricks claude into staying in interactive mode under piped stdio.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary).** The existing `claude-cli` backend is untouched (its predicate `liveSession === "claude-stdio"` matches it alone now); user `agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli` overrides flow through to the interactive backend via `inheritUserConfigFrom` with only `-p`-only flags stripped; the system CA store is **not** mutated (`NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` is scoped to the spawned claude child only); non-`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels (statsig, datadog, etc.) pass through unmodified; tool-use / timestamp / "waiting on tool" injection into the reasoning stream is intentionally **out of scope** here and lands as a separate follow-up to #81550 that rides on the `stream_event` JSONL surface this PR establishes.
- AI-assisted: yes — drafted with Claude.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related: #81550 (programmatic-mode reasoning bridge — this PR introduces the interactive-mode equivalent on a separate backend; the follow-up tool-use injection PR will work on both)
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression
- [x] This PR adds a new backend behind an opt-in backend id

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: `claude` running under OpenClaw in **interactive (subscription) mode** with full `init` / `stream_event*` / `result` JSONL reconstruction on the wrapper's stdout, sourced from the loopback MITM proxy's SSE tap.
- Real environment tested: Windows 11 Pro 26200, Node v24.12.0, pnpm, Bun, `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` v2.1.142 (npm install), Opus 4.7 (`claude-opus-4-7`), live OpenClaw gateway driving the backend per-turn over Telegram.
- Exact steps run after this patch:
  - `unset NODE_OPTIONS && pnpm test:extension anthropic` — focused vitest run of the anthropic extension test files.
  - Set `cliBackends.claude-cli-interactive` as the active backend in user config, restart the OpenClaw gateway, send a Telegram message that triggers an Opus 4.7 turn, observe the wrapper boot in stderr, the API SSE flow through `127.0.0.1:<port>` and arrive at OpenClaw as `stream_event` records.
  - Send a follow-up message with `--resume <sessionId>` (default sessionMode) and verify the second turn picks up the existing claude session.
  - With `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_PROXY_DEBUG=1` set, verify stderr includes `[openclaw-proxy] spawning claude binary: …\node_modules\@anthropic-ai\claude-code\bin\claude.exe` (the npm-installed claude, not a winget-shadowed install).
- Evidence after fix:
  - `pnpm test:extension anthropic` → `Test Files 7 passed (7) / Tests 73 passed (73) / Duration 10.41s`. New `cli-backend-interactive.test.ts` covers the three normalizeConfig branches (override path → `command="bun"` + env passthrough; preserves other env entries; **no override → env passthrough omitted** so wrapper.ts can fall back to a PATH lookup of `claude`).
  - Live Telegram turn: per-turn process emits `init` → `stream_event*` → single `result` on `end_turn`, all sourced from the proxy's SSE tap.
- Observed result after fix: per-turn lifecycle terminates cleanly — wrapper emits `init`, then a continuous stream of `stream_event` records (tapped from the proxy), then a single `result` record on `end_turn`, then sets `intentionalKill = true` and `claude.kill()`s the child so the per-turn process tree exits with code 0.
- **Live capture from the running install** — redacted artifacts on the [`proof-assets/pr-81851`](https://github.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/tree/proof-assets/pr-81851/.proof) branch of my fork:
  - [`.proof/gateway-log-excerpt.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets/pr-81851/.proof/gateway-log-excerpt.txt) — 30 lines from `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log` covering three real per-turn invocations: gateway logs `cli exec: provider=claude-cli-interactive` + `cli argv: bun <NPM>\…\wrapper.ts --effort max --verbose …`, wrapper stderr logs `certs ready` → `MITM proxy active on 127.0.0.1:17081` → `spawning claude binary: <NPM>\…\@anthropic-ai\claude-code\bin\claude.exe` → `result flushed, waiting for session write before kill` → `session file updated, killing claude`. Verbatim, just paths/session-ids redacted.
  - [`.proof/proxy-certs-listing.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets/pr-81851/.proof/proxy-certs-listing.txt) — `~/.openclaw/proxy-certs/` directory listing + `openssl x509 -noout` output for both certs. CA: `CN=OpenClaw Proxy CA`, self-signed, valid `May 14 22:30:14 2026 GMT` → `May 14 22:30:14 2027 GMT`, SHA1 `63:69:13:08:D9:AE:CC:CC:85:D3:A7:B2:AB:E0:A6:06:E3:88:97:2E`. Leaf: `CN=api.anthropic.com`, issued by the CA, SAN `DNS:api.anthropic.com, DNS:statsig.anthropic.com, DNS:console.anthropic.com`.
  - [`.proof/openclaw-config-excerpt.json`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets/pr-81851/.proof/openclaw-config-excerpt.json) — `agents.defaults.model.primary` is `claude-cli-interactive/claude-opus-4-6`; `agents.defaults.models` registers `claude-cli-interactive` runtimes for opus-4-7/4-6/4-5, sonnet-4-6/4-5, haiku-4-5. Production wiring, not a test fixture.

  Embedded sample (one of the three turns in the gateway-log excerpt):

  ```
  cli exec: provider=claude-cli-interactive model=opus promptChars=N trigger=user useResume=true session=present resumeSession=<SESSION> reuse=reusable historyPrompt=present
  cli argv: bun <NPM>\node_modules\openclaw\dist\extensions\anthropic\interactive-proxy\wrapper.ts --effort max --verbose --setting-sources user --permission-mode bypassPermissions --resume <SESSION> --allowedTools mcp__openclaw__* --strict-mcp-config --mcp-config <TMP>\openclaw-cli-mcp-<TMP>\mcp.json --model opus <prompt:N chars>
  [openclaw-proxy] certs ready: <HOME>\.openclaw\proxy-certs\ca.crt
  [openclaw-proxy] MITM proxy active on 127.0.0.1:17081 -- Anthropic API traffic intercepted locally to capture thinking_delta events
  [openclaw-proxy] spawning claude binary: <NPM>\node_modules\@anthropic-ai\claude-code\bin\claude.exe
  [openclaw-proxy] result flushed, waiting for session write before kill, path: <HOME>\.claude\projects\<WORKSPACE>\<SESSION>.jsonl
  [openclaw-proxy] session file updated, killing claude
  [openclaw-proxy] claude exited, code:  signal: SIGTERM intentional: true
  ```
- What was not tested:
  - Linux/macOS (developed and exercised on Windows 11 only); the wrapper has no Windows-specific code beyond the binary-resolution guard, but I have not run it on Linux/macOS.
  - Bundled-MCP `claude-config-file` mode end-to-end with multiple MCP servers active (only the `mcp__openclaw__*` allowlist path was exercised live).
  - Behaviour against an Anthropic-side `429`/`overloaded_error` mid-stream (the wrapper passes the SSE stream through unchanged, so the existing claude error-handling should apply, but I did not force the failure to confirm).

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a feature PR, not a bugfix. The condition this addresses (no thinking visibility on subscription) is a property of `claude -p` (headless) mode, not a regression in OpenClaw.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts` — asserts `normalizeConfig` post-merge shape (forces `command="bun"`, stashes inherited binary on env when an override exists, omits the env when no override exists).
- Scenario the test should lock in: the post-merge normalize step survives both shapes the runtime can produce — (a) user has a `claude-cli` override → `merged.command` is the inherited absolute path, env passthrough must be set; (b) user has no override → `merged.command` stays as the backend's own `"bun"`, env passthrough must be **omitted** so wrapper.ts doesn't try to spawn bun as the claude binary.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the failure mode is config-shape drift inside `normalizeConfig`, fully exercised by calling `backend.normalizeConfig(merged)` synchronously and asserting the output keys. An e2e run would also catch it but cost minutes per run; the unit test covers all three branches in milliseconds and runs in CI on every PR.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A — the file is new with this PR.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A — three new tests added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New backend id: `claude-cli-interactive`. Selectable via `cliBackends.claude-cli-interactive` user config; nothing changes for users on `claude-cli`.
- When the new backend is active, every claude turn spawns a fresh `bun wrapper.ts …` process that boots a loopback MITM proxy on `127.0.0.1:<port>` for the lifetime of that turn. Process is killed on `end_turn`; loopback ports are released.
- Reasoning stream now carries plaintext `thinking_delta` content on the subscription path (previously visible only on the API-key billing path).
- Title-generation auxiliary turns (claude's side request whose only text content is `{"title":"…"}`) are buffered and silently suppressed before the wrapper emits any records to OpenClaw.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```
OpenClaw gateway
  │  spawns one fresh wrapper per turn:
  ▼
bun wrapper.ts [--session-id …] [--resume …] [prompt-as-positional-arg]
  ├─ cert-manager.ts → generate/cache local CA + api.anthropic.com leaf cert
  ├─ mitm-server.ts  → Stage 1: HTTP CONNECT proxy (loopback only)
  │                    Stage 2: Bun TLS server (terminates TLS, re-issues upstream)
  │                    onEvent(evt) tap on every parsed SSE event
  ▼ NODE_OPTIONS="--require <abs>/tty-spoof.cjs"
    HTTPS_PROXY=http://127.0.0.1:<port>
    NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS=<ca.crt>   ← scoped to this claude child only
  ▼
claude.exe (interactive mode, no -p, ink-based TUI rendering)
  │  API SSE → CONNECT → TLS terminate → tap event → forward to claude
  │  TUI stdout → /dev/null (we ignore it)
  ▼
wrapper.ts emits init / stream_event* / result JSONL on its own stdout,
sourced entirely from the tapped SSE — output identical to the shape
`claude -p --output-format stream-json` produces.
```

### Per-turn kill/restart lifecycle (why we don't use the persistent live-session model)

`claude-cli-interactive` does **not** use the persistent `claude-stdio` live-session model that `claude-cli` uses. Each turn = one fresh `bun wrapper.ts …` invocation:

1. Wrapper boots, lazily generates/loads the local CA, starts the MITM proxy on a loopback port.
2. Wrapper emits `{"type":"init","session_id":"…"}` on its stdout.
3. Wrapper spawns claude over a piped stdin; framework stdin is forwarded to `claude.stdin` so OpenClaw's prompt-as-stdin convention still works.
4. SSE events flow through the proxy → wrapper's `onEvent` handler → wrapper emits `{"type":"stream_event","event":…}` JSONL per event.
5. On `message_stop` with `stop_reason === "end_turn"`, wrapper assembles `{"type":"result","session_id":"…","result":"…","is_error":false,"usage":{…}}`, drains it to stdout, sets `intentionalKill = true`, then `claude.kill()`s the child.
6. Wrapper exits 0; loopback ports released.

Why kill on `end_turn` instead of letting claude self-exit: interactive claude doesn't exit after one turn — it's a TUI awaiting further input. The wrapper has to be authoritative about turn boundaries from the SSE side. `intentionalKill` distinguishes this from claude crashing.

### TTY spoof (`interactive-proxy/tty-spoof.cjs`)

Claude refuses interactive (subscription) mode unless stdin/stdout look like a TTY: it falls back to headless mode the moment `process.stdin.isTTY` is undefined or `tty.isatty(0)` returns false. Under OpenClaw it's spawned with piped stdio, so neither is true. `tty-spoof.cjs` is preloaded into the claude child via `NODE_OPTIONS=--require <abs-path>` and patches:

- `process.stdin.isTTY`, `process.stdout.isTTY`, `process.stderr.isTTY` → `true` (property level).
- `tty.isatty(fd)` → returns `true` for fd 0/1/2 (the ink renderer calls this directly via libuv, bypassing the property).
- `process.stdin.setRawMode` → no-op stub (ink calls this; would throw on a pipe).
- `process.stdout.columns`/`rows` and `process.stderr.columns`/`rows` → defaulted to 200×50 so ink layout has dimensions to render against.

Minimum surface needed for claude to enter and stay in interactive mode under piped stdio. Nothing else is patched — claude still does its real work, makes real API calls, and emits its real TUI bytes (we discard them).

### Full JSONL reconstruction (backwards compatibility with `claude -p --output-format stream-json`)

OpenClaw's JSONL parser expects exactly the output shape of `claude -p --output-format stream-json`. The wrapper reproduces it record-for-record, sourced entirely from the proxy:

- **`init`** (session_id) — emitted on wrapper boot, before claude even spawns.
- **`stream_event`** (one per tapped SSE event) — forwarded verbatim from the proxy: `message_start`, `content_block_start`, `content_block_delta` (`text_delta` / `thinking_delta` / `signature_delta` / `tool_use`), `content_block_stop`, `message_delta`, `message_stop`, `ping`. The proxy emits everything; the wrapper emits everything; OpenClaw's JSONL parser sees the same shape it would have on the `-p` path.
- **`result`** (one per turn) — synthesised on `end_turn` from accumulated text-delta concatenation + final usage from `message_delta`.

The proxy is a thin pass-through — it tees every parsed SSE event to a single `onEvent` handler and otherwise forwards the SSE stream unmodified to claude. No event-specific logic lives in the proxy; the wrapper interprets everything. To avoid wasted CPU on auxiliary turns the proxy only taps SSE responses for requests that contain a non-empty `tools` array (real conversation turns); title-gen and other no-tool requests pass through without inspection.

### Binary resolution

`normalizeConfig` runs after `mergeBackendConfig` shallow-spreads the inherited `claude-cli` override on top of this backend's base config. The merge replaces `command` with the inherited absolute claude binary path. The normalize step:

- Forces `command` back to `"bun"` so the wrapper actually runs as a script (otherwise `claude.exe` would be spawned with `wrapper.ts` as a positional and the proxy would never start).
- Stashes the inherited binary path on `env.OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_CLAUDE_BINARY` so `wrapper.ts` spawns that exact binary instead of doing a PATH lookup. PATH lookups on Windows resolve via PATHEXT to whichever `claude.exe` arrived first — a winget install can shadow the npm install OpenClaw configured.
- **Guards the no-override case**: if `merged.command` is still our backend's own `"bun"` (no `claude-cli` override took effect), the env passthrough is **omitted** so `wrapper.ts` falls back to a PATH lookup of `"claude"` rather than trying to spawn bun as the claude binary.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **Yes** — a per-turn local TLS MITM on a loopback port. Scoped to the spawned claude child only via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS`; system trust store is not modified. Local CA + leaf private key are generated lazily on first use and cached under the user's OpenClaw config dir. The CA exists solely to issue a single `api.anthropic.com` leaf cert that the per-turn child trusts via the env var; no other process consumes it.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No** — the wrapper does not read, log, or modify the Anthropic auth token. It is forwarded transparently in the request that the proxy re-issues upstream over real TLS.
- New/changed network calls? **Yes** — claude's outbound HTTPS to `api.anthropic.com:443` is rerouted via `HTTPS_PROXY=http://127.0.0.1:<port>` inside the child. The proxy CONNECTs to the real upstream and reissues each request unmodified; the request body is read once to detect the `tools` field for the SSE-tap decision and is forwarded byte-for-byte. Non-`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels (statsig, datadog, etc.) bypass the TLS terminator entirely.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No** — same `claude` binary, same allowed-tools set (`mcp__openclaw__*`), same `--setting-sources user`, same `--session-id` semantics.
- Data access scope changed? **No** — the proxy sees only the SSE bytes claude itself sees on the wire; nothing is exfiltrated, persisted, or aggregated outside the per-turn process.

Risk + mitigation summary for the Yes items: the trust boundary changes for Anthropic traffic of *this child process only*. Mitigation: child-scoped `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS`, no system CA mutation, CA private key restricted to the user's OpenClaw config dir, no other process configured to trust it. Backend is opt-in by id (`claude-cli-interactive`), not a default. The whole reason for this surface is to keep OpenClaw on the interactive subscription pool after the June 15 split — the `-p` (programmatic) path that doesn't intercept TLS remains the default, and users keep that experience by selecting the existing `claude-cli` backend.

## Prior art / alternatives considered

- [`smithersai/claude-p`](https://github.com/smithersai/claude-p) takes a different approach to keeping Claude on the interactive subscription pool: it runs `claude` without `-p` and bypasses the JSONL streaming surface entirely. That works for the raw final text, but **loses the JSONL `stream_event` records that any reasoning stream or tool-use rendering depends on**. The JSONL-reconstruction approach in this PR (re-emitting the same record shape `claude -p --output-format stream-json` produces, sourced from the SSE tap) preserves the existing OpenClaw stream consumer unchanged — so the existing reasoning bridge and the tool-use surface in the follow-up PR layer on cleanly without losing fidelity.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** — the existing `claude-cli` backend is unchanged. Adding the new backend is opt-in via the `claude-cli-interactive` id; no default flips.
- Config/env changes? **No** — no new required user config. New optional env var `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_PROXY_DEBUG=1` enables verbose stderr from the wrapper.
- Migration needed? **No**.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a newly-installed `claude-cli` user with no inherited override could end up spawning `bun` as the claude binary if `normalizeConfig` blindly stores `merged.command` in the env passthrough.
  - Mitigation: `normalizeConfig` skips the env passthrough when `merged.command === "bun"`; the wrapper falls back to a PATH lookup of `"claude"`. Regression test in `cli-backend-interactive.test.ts` asserts the env var is `undefined` in that branch.
- Risk: the `tty-spoof.cjs` preload is a runtime support file, not a build output, so it could be omitted from packaged installs and break interactive mode at runtime with `MODULE_NOT_FOUND`.
  - Mitigation: the file is declared in `extensions/anthropic/package.json#openclaw.build.staticAssets` so the postbuild asset copier ships it alongside the wrapper, mitm-server, and cert-manager.
- Risk: a winget-installed `claude.exe` shadows the npm install on Windows because `spawn("claude")` resolves via PATHEXT, leading to `unknown option '--effort'` on older claude versions.
  - Mitigation: when an override is present, `wrapper.ts` spawns the exact binary the parent backend resolved (passed via `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_CLAUDE_BINARY`) instead of falling back to a bare PATH lookup.
- Risk: title-generation side requests can land mid-turn and inject `{"title":"…"}` artifacts into the reasoning stream.
  - Mitigation: the wrapper buffers early `text_delta`s when the accumulated text starts with `{`; if it parses as JSON with a `title` field the entire turn is suppressed before any record is emitted to OpenClaw. Defence-in-depth: the proxy already skips SSE tapping for requests without a `tools` array, so most auxiliary requests never reach the wrapper at all.
- Risk: TLS interception persists a local CA + leaf key under the user's OpenClaw config dir indefinitely; an attacker with read access to that directory could craft a leaf cert trusted by the per-turn child.
  - Mitigation: trust is scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` (no other process opts in), the system trust store is unchanged, and the CA exists solely to issue the one `api.anthropic.com` leaf the per-turn proxy needs. Maintainer security review explicitly invited as part of this PR.
